  • About This Report

  • Primary Analyses
  • Results Summary
  • Reproducibility Information

Communication Responsiveness and Relationship Satisfaction in Long-Distance Relationships

Author

Zhiyi Cui

About This Report

This reproducible report documents the data preparation and statistical analysis for our PSY 120L group research project. The report shows how the raw Qualtrics dataset was cleaned, how the main variables were scored, and how the final analyses were conducted in R.

Study Overview

This study examined whether perceived communication responsiveness affects relationship satisfaction in an imagined long-distance romantic relationship. Participants read either a high-responsiveness or low-responsiveness vignette. Relationship satisfaction was measured using the Relationship Assessment Scale. We also examined whether the effect of responsiveness differed based on attachment anxiety and prior long-distance relationship experience.

The primary analyses were two 2 × 2 between-subjects ANOVAs:

  1. Communication Responsiveness × Attachment Anxiety on Relationship Satisfaction

  2. Communication Responsiveness × Prior LDR Experience on Relationship Satisfaction

Setup and Software Environment

The report first sets global chunk options and loads the packages used for data preparation, descriptive statistics, ANOVAs, estimated means, and figures.

Code 
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE
)

options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

load packages

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)
library(afex)
library(emmeans)

Data Source and Analysis Dataset

The raw Qualtrics file was imported from the local data folder.

Read in Your Data

Code 
data <- read_csv("data/LongDistanceRelationship.csv")

dim(data)
[1] 226  39
Code 
names(data)
 [1] "StartDate"             "EndDate"               "Duration (in seconds)"
 [4] "Finished"              "RecordedDate"          "Consent Form"         
 [7] "MC1"                   "RAS1"                  "RAS2"                 
[10] "RAS3"                  "RAS4"                  "RAS5"                 
[13] "RAS6"                  "RAS7"                  "AAS1"                 
[16] "AAS2"                  "AAS3"                  "AAS4"                 
[19] "AAS5"                  "AAS6"                  "AAS7"                 
[22] "AAS8"                  "AAS9"                  "AAS10"                
[25] "AAS11"                 "AAS12"                 "AAS13"                
[28] "AAS14"                 "AAS15"                 "AAS16"                
[31] "AAS17"                 "AAS18"                 "Age"                  
[34] "Age_8_TEXT"            "Gender"                "Gender _5_TEXT"       
[37] "Relationship_exp"      "Relationship_length"   "Condition"
Code 
head(data)

Clean Raw Data

Code 
study_data <- data %>%
  slice(-1, -2)

Variable Preparation

This section identifies the variables needed for the analyses, recodes the experimental condition, computes scale scores, checks missingness, creates grouping variables, and summarizes internal consistency.

Select and Verify Analysis Dataset

Only the variables needed for scoring, descriptive statistics, and planned analyses were retained. Numeric conversion was applied to the survey items and demographic codes.

Code 
study_vars <- c(
  "Condition",
  "MC1",
  "RAS1", "RAS2", "RAS3", "RAS4", "RAS5", "RAS6", "RAS7",
  "AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS3", "AAS4", "AAS5", "AAS6",
  "AAS7", "AAS8", "AAS9", "AAS10", "AAS11", "AAS12",
  "AAS13", "AAS14", "AAS15", "AAS16", "AAS17", "AAS18",
  "Age",
  "Gender",
  "Relationship_exp",
  "Relationship_length"
)
Code 
length(study_vars)
[1] 31
Code 
study_data <- study_data[, study_vars]

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    across(-Condition, as.numeric),
    Condition = factor(Condition)
  )

summary(study_data)

Recode Communication Responsiveness Condition

Condition labels were changed from short codes to reader-friendly labels for tables and figures.

Code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    Condition = factor(
      Condition,
      levels = c("Low", "High"),
      labels = c("Low Responsiveness",
                 "High Responsiveness")
    )
  )

study_data %>%
  count(Condition)

Create Participant ID

A row-based participant ID was created so that each response could be used as a unique case in the ANOVA models.

Code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(id = row_number())
study_data %>%
  select(id) %>%
  head()

Create and Check Relationship Satisfaction Items

RAS items 4 and 7 were reverse-coded before the seven items were averaged. Higher scores indicate greater relationship satisfaction.

Code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    RAS4r = 6 - RAS4,
    RAS7r = 6 - RAS7
  )

ras_items <- c("RAS1", "RAS2", "RAS3", "RAS4r", "RAS5", "RAS6", "RAS7r")

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    relationship_satisfaction = rowMeans(across(all_of(ras_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

summary(study_data$relationship_satisfaction)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.     NAs 
  1.000   2.571   3.286   3.292   4.000   5.000      53

Define Attachment Subscales

The Adult Attachment Scale items were grouped into attachment-anxiety and attachment-avoidance subscales. Higher scores indicate greater attachment insecurity.

Code 
anxiety_items <- c(
  "AAS3", "AAS4", "AAS9",
  "AAS10", "AAS11", "AAS15"
)

avoidance_items <- c(
  "AAS1", "AAS2", "AAS5", "AAS6",
  "AAS7", "AAS8", "AAS12", "AAS13",
  "AAS14", "AAS16", "AAS17", "AAS18"
)

setNames(anxiety_items %in% names(study_data), anxiety_items)
 AAS3  AAS4  AAS9 AAS10 AAS11 AAS15 
 TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
Code 
setNames(avoidance_items %in% names(study_data), avoidance_items)
 AAS1  AAS2  AAS5  AAS6  AAS7  AAS8 AAS12 AAS13 AAS14 AAS16 AAS17 AAS18 
 TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE

Compute Attachment Scores

Composite scores were created by averaging the relevant items for each participant.

Code 
study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    relationship_satisfaction =
      rowMeans(across(all_of(ras_items)), na.rm = TRUE),

    attachment_anxiety =
      rowMeans(across(all_of(anxiety_items)), na.rm = TRUE),

    attachment_avoidance =
      rowMeans(across(all_of(avoidance_items)), na.rm = TRUE)
  )

summary(study_data$relationship_satisfaction)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.     NAs 
  1.000   2.571   3.286   3.292   4.000   5.000      53
Code 
summary(study_data$attachment_anxiety)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.     NAs 
  1.333   2.500   2.833   2.853   3.167   4.333      56
Code 
summary(study_data$attachment_avoidance)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.     NAs 
  1.917   2.833   3.000   3.024   3.250   4.333      56

Check Missingness

Missingness was checked before running the analyses so that the final model sample sizes could be interpreted clearly.

Code 
mean(is.na(study_data$relationship_satisfaction))
[1] 0.2366071
Code 
mean(is.na(study_data$attachment_anxiety))
[1] 0.25
Code 
mean(is.na(study_data$attachment_avoidance))
[1] 0.25

Create Attachment Anxiety Groups

Attachment anxiety was divided at the sample median to create low-anxiety and high-anxiety groups for the planned 2 × 2 ANOVA.

Code 
anxiety_median <- median(
  study_data$attachment_anxiety,
  na.rm = TRUE
)

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    anxiety_msplit = if_else(
      attachment_anxiety <= anxiety_median,
      "Low Anxiety",
      "High Anxiety"
    )
  )

study_data <- study_data %>%
  mutate(
    anxiety_msplit = factor(
      anxiety_msplit,
      levels = c(
        "Low Anxiety",
        "High Anxiety"
      )
    )
  )

study_data %>%
  count(anxiety_msplit) %>%
  knitr::kable()
anxiety_msplit n
Low Anxiety 99
High Anxiety 69
NA 56

Check Reliability

Internal consistency was checked for the relationship-satisfaction scale and attachment subscales using Cronbach’s alpha.

Code 
psych::alpha(
  study_data %>%
    select(all_of(ras_items))
)

Reliability analysis   
Call: psych::alpha(x = study_data %>% select(all_of(ras_items)))

  raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r S/N    ase mean sd median_r
      0.92      0.92    0.92      0.61  11 0.0081  3.3  1     0.54

    95% confidence boundaries 
         lower alpha upper
Feldt      0.9  0.92  0.93
Duhachek   0.9  0.92  0.93

 Reliability if an item is dropped:
      raw_alpha std.alpha G6(smc) average_r  S/N alpha se var.r med.r

RAS3       0.89      0.89    0.90      0.57  8.1   0.0108 0.024  0.54

RAS5       0.90      0.89    0.90      0.58  8.2   0.0105 0.027  0.52
RAS6       0.92      0.91    0.92      0.64 10.8   0.0085 0.027  0.54
RAS7r      0.92      0.92    0.92      0.66 11.4   0.0078 0.026  0.60

Code 
psych::alpha(
  study_data %>%
    select(all_of(anxiety_items))
)
Some items ( AAS3 AAS4 ) were negatively correlated with the first principal component and 
probably should be reversed.  
To do this, run the function again with the 'check.keys=TRUE' option

Reliability analysis   
Call: psych::alpha(x = study_data %>% select(all_of(anxiety_items)))

Descriptive Statistics

Descriptive statistics summarize the main continuous variables and the sizes of the categorical groups used in the analyses.

Code 
psych::describe(
  study_data %>%
    select(MC1, relationship_satisfaction, attachment_anxiety, attachment_avoidance)
)
Code 
study_data %>%
  count(Condition)

Continuous Variables

Code 
psych::describe(
  study_data %>%
    select(
      relationship_satisfaction,
      attachment_anxiety,
      attachment_avoidance
    )
) %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
vars n mean sd median trimmed mad min max range skew kurtosis se
relationship_satisfaction 1 171 3.29 1.01 3.29 3.30 1.06 1.00 5.00 4.00 -0.04 -0.96 0.08
attachment_anxiety 2 168 2.85 0.46 2.83 2.84 0.49 1.33 4.33 3.00 0.34 0.90 0.04
attachment_avoidance 3 168 3.02 0.36 3.00 3.03 0.37 1.92 4.33 2.42 -0.15 1.38 0.03

Categorical Variables

Code 
study_data %>%
  count(Condition) %>%
  knitr::kable()
Condition n
Low Responsiveness 109
High Responsiveness 107
NA 8
Code 
study_data %>%
  count(anxiety_msplit) %>%
  knitr::kable()
anxiety_msplit n
Low Anxiety 99
High Anxiety 69
NA 56

Manipulation Check

The manipulation check tested whether participants perceived the partner as more responsive in the high-responsiveness condition than in the low-responsiveness condition.

Code 
mc_summary <- study_data %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(Condition),
    !is.na(MC1)
  ) %>%
  group_by(Condition) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    M = mean(MC1),
    SD = sd(MC1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

mc_summary %>%
  knitr::kable(digits = 2)
Condition n M SD
Low Responsiveness 97 2.67 1.25
High Responsiveness 91 4.85 1.23
Code 
t.test(
  MC1 ~ Condition,
  data = study_data
)

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  MC1 by Condition
t = -12.046, df = 185.56, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group Low Responsiveness and group High Responsiveness is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -2.532447 -1.819654
sample estimates:
 mean in group Low Responsiveness mean in group High Responsiveness 
                         2.670103                          4.846154

Participants in the high-responsiveness condition rated the partner as more responsive (M = 4.85, SD = 1.23) than participants in the low-responsiveness condition (M = 2.67, SD = 1.25), t(185.56) = -12.05, p < .001. This indicates that the vignette manipulation worked as intended.

Primary Analyses

Two 2 × 2 between-subjects ANOVAs tested whether communication responsiveness was associated with relationship satisfaction and whether this pattern differed across the two measured moderators.

Analysis 1: Communication Responsiveness × Attachment Group

The first ANOVA tested whether communication responsiveness and attachment group were related to relationship satisfaction. Participants in the high-responsiveness condition reported higher satisfaction than participants in the low-responsiveness condition. The attachment-group main effect and interaction should be described based on the finalized attachment analysis.

Code 
a1 <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "relationship_satisfaction",
  between = c("Condition", "anxiety_msplit"),
  data = study_data
)

a1
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: relationship_satisfaction
                    Effect     df  MSE          F   ges p.value
1                Condition 1, 164 0.61 114.14 ***  .410   <.001
2           anxiety_msplit 1, 164 0.61       0.28  .002    .598
3 Condition:anxiety_msplit 1, 164 0.61       0.14 <.001    .709
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

Communication responsiveness had a significant main effect, F(1, 164) = 114.14, p < .001, η²G = .410. Participants reported higher satisfaction in the high-responsiveness condition (M = 3.94, 95% CI [3.77, 4.11]) than in the low-responsiveness condition (M = 2.63, 95% CI [2.46, 2.80]). Attachment anxiety did not have a significant main effect, F(1, 164) = 0.28, p = .598, η²G = .002, and the interaction was not significant, F(1, 164) = 0.14, p = .709, η²G < .001. The responsiveness pattern was similar for both attachment-anxiety groups.

Marginal Means

Code 
emmeans(a1, ~ Condition)
 Condition           emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low Responsiveness    2.63 0.0879 164     2.46     2.80
 High Responsiveness   3.94 0.0853 164     3.77     4.11

Results are averaged over the levels of: anxiety_msplit 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Code 
emmeans(a1, ~ anxiety_msplit)
 anxiety_msplit emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low Anxiety      3.32 0.0784 164     3.16     3.47
 High Anxiety     3.25 0.0941 164     3.07     3.44

Results are averaged over the levels of: Condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95

Cell Means

Code 
emmeans(a1, ~ Condition * anxiety_msplit)
 Condition           anxiety_msplit emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low Responsiveness  Low Anxiety      2.69 0.109 164     2.47     2.90
 High Responsiveness Low Anxiety      3.95 0.113 164     3.73     4.17
 Low Responsiveness  High Anxiety     2.58 0.138 164     2.30     2.85
 High Responsiveness High Anxiety     3.93 0.128 164     3.68     4.18

Confidence level used: 0.95

Interaction Plot

Code 
p_bar <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(
    emmeans(
      a1,
      ~ Condition * anxiety_msplit
    )
  ),
  aes(
    x = Condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = anxiety_msplit
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction",
    fill = "Attachment Anxiety"
  ) +
  theme_classic()

print(p_bar)

Figure 1. The graph shows a similar responsiveness pattern for participants with low and high attachment anxiety. In both groups, satisfaction was higher in the high-responsiveness condition.

Save Figure

Code 
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  "figures/interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_bar,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

Analysis 2: Communication Responsiveness × Prior LDR Experience

The second ANOVA tested whether communication responsiveness and prior LDR experience were related to relationship satisfaction. Participants in the high-responsiveness condition reported higher satisfaction than participants in the low-responsiveness condition. Prior LDR experience was not significantly related to satisfaction, and it did not significantly change the effect of responsiveness.

Code 
a2 <- afex::aov_ez(
  id = "id",
  dv = "relationship_satisfaction",
  data = study_data,
  between = c(
    "Condition",
    "Relationship_exp"
  )
)

a2
Anova Table (Type 3 tests)

Response: relationship_satisfaction
                      Effect     df  MSE         F  ges p.value
1                  Condition 1, 161 0.61 98.41 *** .379   <.001
2           Relationship_exp 1, 161 0.61      0.23 .001    .631
3 Condition:Relationship_exp 1, 161 0.61      1.78 .011    .184
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '+' 0.1 ' ' 1

Communication responsiveness had a significant main effect, F(1, 161) = 98.41, p < .001, η²G = .379. Participants reported higher satisfaction in the high-responsiveness condition (M = 3.89, 95% CI [3.72, 4.07]) than in the low-responsiveness condition (M = 2.65, 95% CI [2.48, 2.83]). Prior LDR experience did not have a significant main effect, F(1, 161) = 0.23, p = .631, η²G = .001, and the interaction was not significant, F(1, 161) = 1.78, p = .184, η²G = .011. The responsiveness pattern was similar for participants with and without prior LDR experience.

Marginal Means

Code 
emmeans(a2, ~ Condition)
 Condition           emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low Responsiveness    2.65 0.0866 161     2.48     2.83
 High Responsiveness   3.89 0.0901 161     3.72     4.07

Results are averaged over the levels of: Relationship_exp 
Confidence level used: 0.95
Code 
emmeans(a2, ~ Relationship_exp)
 Relationship_exp emmean     SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 0                  3.24 0.0950 161     3.06     3.43
 1                  3.30 0.0812 161     3.14     3.46

Results are averaged over the levels of: Condition 
Confidence level used: 0.95

Cell Means

Code 
cell_means_exp <- emmeans(
  a2,
  ~ Condition * Relationship_exp
)

cell_means_exp
 Condition           Relationship_exp emmean    SE  df lower.CL upper.CL
 Low Responsiveness  0                  2.71 0.120 161     2.47     2.94
 High Responsiveness 0                  3.78 0.147 161     3.49     4.07
 Low Responsiveness  1                  2.60 0.125 161     2.35     2.85
 High Responsiveness 1                  4.01 0.104 161     3.80     4.21

Confidence level used: 0.95

Interaction Plot

Code 
p_exp <- ggplot(
  as.data.frame(cell_means_exp),
  aes(
    x = Condition,
    y = emmean,
    fill = Relationship_exp
  )
) +
  geom_col(
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.55,
    color = "white"
  ) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(
      ymin = lower.CL,
      ymax = upper.CL
    ),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.65),
    width = 0.12,
    linewidth = 0.7
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Communication Responsiveness Condition",
    y = "Mean Relationship Satisfaction",
    fill = "Previous LDR Experience"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 14)

print(p_exp)

Figure 2. The graph shows a similar responsiveness pattern for participants with and without prior LDR experience. In both groups, satisfaction was higher in the high-responsiveness condition.

Save Interaction Figure

Code 
if (!dir.exists("figures")) {
  dir.create("figures")
}

ggsave(
  "figures/ldr_experience_interaction_plot.svg",
  plot = p_exp,
  width = 7,
  height = 5
)

Results Summary

The analyses showed that communication responsiveness was consistently related to relationship satisfaction. Participants who read the high-responsiveness vignette rated the imagined relationship more positively than participants who read the low-responsiveness vignette.

The first analysis examined whether this effect differed based on attachment anxiety. Communication responsiveness had a significant effect on relationship satisfaction. However, attachment anxiety did not have a significant main effect, and the interaction was not significant. This means that participants reported higher satisfaction in the high-responsiveness condition regardless of whether their attachment anxiety was low or high.

The second analysis examined whether this effect differed based on prior long-distance relationship experience. Communication responsiveness again had a significant effect on relationship satisfaction. Prior LDR experience did not have a significant main effect, and the interaction was not significant. This suggests that responsive communication was associated with higher satisfaction for participants with and without prior LDR experience.

Overall, the findings supported the hypothesis that high communication responsiveness would be related to greater relationship satisfaction. The moderation hypotheses were not supported.

Reproducibility Information

This report was created using Quarto and R. The dataset is not published because it contains participant-level responses. The report includes the code used to import the dataset, prepare variables, calculate scale scores, run the analyses, and generate the figures.
